speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Greenman in London, England, and this is tape number 7. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was October, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I said to the young man, go there. You'll find Mr. Greenman. Tell Mr. Greenman that his son will be in in about 10 minutes' time. I didn't want to jump in straightaway. I wanted to prepare them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got in, my father was very thin-- very thin, starved. I saw the pictures on the wall with my wife and child. Then I had a damn good cry. And I went out the next morning. I got up early. Father said, what are you getting up early for? I've got to get out. I've got no rest. I want to see who and whatnot. I couldn't find any family. I couldn't find friends. One woman I found who I knew from years ago-- she used to dance. I used to sing. She said to me-- and I told where I had been. She said, did you see my husband? I said, I didn't know you had a husband. Yes, she says. His name I don't know. I said I saw many people, and I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, you're coming home for a cup of coffee, cup of tea. I said, all right. I came home, to her home. And the door, the [? sea ?] door, as it opens, you're inside the room. And on the dresser, I saw a photograph of-- I said, oh, I know him, they shot him, without thinking. She said, that's my husband. Well, I knew him very well in the camp. He was the [GERMAN] in Auschwitz. And he was evacuated. He had to go to another camp. And a couple of days after that, I heard [GERMAN] was shot-- was shot. Probably ran away, tried to run away. Yeah. And I didn't know. She married now again, this woman. Well, I went looking. I couldn't find. There was a bureau that gave you every week some money. People came back from the camps could get some money. There was an advert in the paper in the Red Cross. People coming back from the camp could apply for fresh milk, extra milk. I applied for extra milk, thinking I'll give it to my father. This was in October, 1945. Two months later, when I was in England, December-- you can see the letter afterwards-- it says, as you are not a Dutch national, you're British, we cannot let you have any milk. That's the Red Cross. The Red Cross won't get any penny from me anymore. They did nothing in the war for us, and they did nothing after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, Dad, we-- my father was out of Westerbork. He was home, so my stepmother was there. I said, we better go back to London. There's nobody here, no one-- the family all gone. And I went to the bureau for money. I said, could I have enough money for three fares to London? We don't want any more money. And we went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the 22nd November, 1945, we arrived at Gravesend at Tilbury docks. My brothers had been in the army. Morry had been in the Second World War. Charlie had been in the First World War, but was a home guard in the Second World War. They met us at Gravesend. And then I stayed with my brother, [INAUDIBLE] in his house for a while. He took me to the doctors because I wasn't well. And Dr. Phillips in [INAUDIBLE], somewhere, he had his surgery, saw to my health. I became better. I blew up. I was very, very heavy for nothing. [INAUDIBLE] water come down. And then came the time-- the government allowed me 25 shillings a week. I couldn't do nothing with that. After that, they increased it to 10 [? bob ?] more, 35 shillings. I had no clothes. I said, only give me enough money to buy a coat and coupons-- everything was in coupons-- and the rest, you can keep. I don't want any more money. So I got a long coat. I could go out in the cold. And the markets-- I went in the markets with a little [INAUDIBLE] case selling little items, little figures, for [INAUDIBLE]. And I remember going to one of the markets, Finsbury-- Finsbury near Mount Pleasant Post Office. And the inspector said that this was an orange box and a little [INAUDIBLE] case. Put your work from that pitch. And I put it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to me was a man selling greens and groceries, a man about 60. He said, oy, what are you doing there? I said, what do you mean? Well, I've never worked in the markets. I said, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you can't work there. I said, well, the inspector said I could stand here. He said, get back to Palestine. That's where you belong. That was the first anti-Semitic thing I heard. I've just come from the lot. How stupid this man was. Well, so the market-- there was nothing else I could do. I went to a Jewish organization in East London. I wanted a loan of 500 pounds so I could start my book business again. They let me wait a fortnight. When I came back, they said we only can let you have 50 pounds, but you must sign for it. We guarantee that you paid us back in three months. I said, look here. 50 pounds won't help me. I can buy a load of rubbishy books, or I might buy one book, and then I've got to go out and find a customer for it. So I can't guarantee three months repay. Keep the money. I'll see to myself. And I didn't have a penny [INAUDIBLE]. And I went on the markets. And I've been over 40 years [? London ?] markets, outside Wales, Midlands, North, until I retired a few years ago. And that's how I got my living, behind the stores in the markets. I haven't married again. It appears I had enough-- not enough money. I had no car and I had no house. So the women were looking for a guarantee, and I didn't have it. So I didn't marry again. It's been partly a lonely life, but I don't tell the outside world. But that won't help me. I'm still alive. I don't know how long for yet. Time is getting on. And that's it. That's my-- what I still do. Now, after the markets, I retired early. At 60, I retired. I had to do something. I still was studying my singing. I forgot to mention it, but, after the war, I went into show business. I appeared in the Dutch television and radio until I came to England. And in England, of course, I met again authority, and you had to do this, you had to do that, before you could do it. Well, you see it up there, the photographs. I had a little taste of show business. My height was against me. Otherwise, I probably would've been a Tom Jones or something like that. But I'm not a pop singer. I'm a romantic singer of ballads. And I gave that up because I wanted to continue with my lecture work on the Holocaust. My first lecture was already in 1946, a few months after I came back out of the camps, for the Maccabee Club in the East End of London. And then I had to look for a living. So during the day, I was in the markets, and during the night, I went singing in the working man's clubs to earn a few quid here and a few quid there. And then I made up my mind to retire and to go into the schools and universities and colleges, telling them about the Holocaust. And I did. And I still do. And I've been doing this over 25 years. Thousands of youths know and have heard me. And I'm still fighting Nazism after 50 years. That's what I'm doing now. And next week, I'm going to Edinburgh. And a week after, I'll be in Lancaster. I'm booked up in February and March already. But I'm thinking of giving up the rest of next year, because I want to live a bit. I want to have my own time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you've done a wonderful job with the lecturing and informing people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message that you would like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Future generation? I hope that the future generation, wherever they are, use their own thoughts for the mankind and goodness of the world. Don't let them believe everything they hear said by governments. Examine it for yourself. But certainly, stay away from fascism, racism, Nazism. In other words, stay away from hate. It's not necessary. Whether you're white, whether you're black, whether you're yellow, you are a human being. You haven't asked to come into this world. You were made. And while you're here, do the right thing. Create love, understanding. That will create happiness. And I hope one day, there will be no more war, and everybody will have sufficient to live a happy life. That's my dream. I hope it can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE] Would you like to tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Dutch grandfather and grandmother. Joseph Broenteman, who went to America and had difficulty that the custom officers, or whoever they were, couldn't translate the name Broenteman. And [? Broen ?] meant green, and man is man, so they made him Greenman. That's why my father's name is Barney Greenman, and I'm Greenman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- that's the only one I've got. The original one is in a museum. This is my White Russian grandfather, from mother's side. This is my father as a young man. This is my father as well. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So just describe this picture, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This? [POINTS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, Leon. Don't point to it, because when you-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my-- my Russian-- White Russian grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, he needs to-- he needs to look at the monitor [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at the monitor. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that's my Russian grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: From mother's side, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Clara Morris Greenman. I was 2 years and 1/2 when she died. According to medicine people, she ought to have brought one child into the world, and she brought six into the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Dinah, my sister Dinah. Now, Dinah and Morry, when they were young, and father couldn't cope with six children, they went into an orphan home. At 18, they came out of the orphan home. Morry was then a tailor and went to England. Dinah [? paired ?] up with a family, Cohen, in the same street where we used to live, and became very friendly with the daughters of that family, probably over 30 or 40 years-- a hell of a long time. The family Cohen-- one of the daughters was called up with her husband and two children to go to Auschwitz. And Dinah said, you can't leave me alone behind. You're going to Auschwitz. Whether I work in Auschwitz or I work in Rotterdam, I'm going with you to Auschwitz. I can work there as well. And voluntary, she went, without knowing what was going to happen. She was in Westerbork. I remember we got a card from her out of Westerbork telling us soon she would be sent to Auschwitz. And naturally, you know now what happened. She didn't return. That was Dinah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my wife, Elsa, Esther [? Van Damme ?]. How old was she in that picture? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] remember? How old she was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: About 27, 28. This is my wife, our son Barney, and myself. This was taken in 1942, probably a month or two before we were taken away to Auschwitz, in the garden of non-Jewish people who were formerly neighbors of us. The lady in question is still alive and lives in Rotterdam. Shortly after this picture was taken, the Nazis forbade the Jews to visit non-Jewish houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you like to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the [DUTCH] in Rotterdam, the street where I used to live. And this is a picture out of a monthly or weekly booklet of 1928. And it shows on there people I knew. And I don't think there's anybody of them alive. That's Barney, about 2 years of age on that picture-- blonde curls, blue eyes, red cheeks, and strong as a lion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was murdered. He wasn't quite 3 years of age-- 2 years and 10 months-- when the Nazis put him in the gas chamber with his mother. This is Kurt Schlesinger, the German Jew who came out of Germany, probably in 1930, '32, '33, became chief administrator in Westerbork and sent us to Auschwitz. And I accuse him of being guilty of my wife and child being murdered in the gas chambers. He didn't look in the mail the morning that we were sent away. He did it after. A very, very bad man. He was-- This is the identity card. You see the letter J on it-- Jew. You go to the town hall, and they register you. You got to carry that on you. So on the streets, anybody wants to see your papers, you show them that and they can see you're  a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this issued?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. Towards the end of 1940, when the Nazis came in-- one of the first laws they made. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] See, the non-Jew had no J on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's me, a guide to schools, colleges, whether they're Dutch or English, at Birkenau and Auschwitz, showing them around and telling them what happened to our folk while I was there. You see here a plaque on the ground which says that 4 million people were killed here by the Nazis. Then the Polish government, after a while, said there were no 4 million. There were probably a million or a little more. So they altered that. And at present, you'll find now the plaques with 1 million something on it. Living at present behind wired windows. [INAUDIBLE] There are nine or 10 windows in the house, and every one is now wired up, framed, so when the Nazis want to throw a brick, it won't touch the glass. In June, two years ago, they threw a brick, and I've got the brick over there. And they smashed the glass, 3 foot by 5 foot glass, and told me that next time, there'll be worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here is a picture of mine. This was taken after camp life in France. I had my goods with me. And the people who took me to the outhouse, summer house, they wanted to see me, what I looked like in camp. And they made a picture of it, as you see. There's my wife. That's me and the baby. [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Leon, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I have in here is some ashes. When Buchenwald liberated and I was free to roam about, I found myself in front of the ovens. I looked into the ovens, and I saw there remains-- ashes, bones-- of people. And when I turned around, on my other side, the left-hand side of mine, there stood four large, wooden containers, really filled to the top with ashes. And I couldn't help taking some of the ashes and saving them. This-- these are the remains of some victim.
